slides.title: Who Owns the Bits?
slides.subtitle: Intellectual property in a copy-paste world — plagiarism, the three legal routes to reuse, and where computing turns harmful
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Topic 5.5, Legal and Ethical Concerns — one day, one learning objective. The Internet made copying effortless, and that single fact created most of today's content: who owns digital material, what plagiarism is, and the three legal routes — Creative Commons, open source, and open access — that let creators share on purpose. The topic then widens past copyright to computing used to harm people, computing in politics, and the digital divide. By the end of the period students can explain, in CED language, how the use of computing raises legal and ethical concerns — exactly what LO IOC-1.F asks.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how ease of access to digitized material raises intellectual property concerns, and what makes a use plagiarism (LO IOC-1.F)
Match a reuse of someone else's material to Creative Commons, open source, or open access — and explain why citing is still required (LO IOC-1.F)
Identify legal and ethical concerns computing raises beyond copyright — harm, political use, the digital divide, and monitoring (LO IOC-1.F)

slides.script: Three targets, all inside IOC-1.F. First, the property story: material made on a computer belongs to somebody, easy copying threatens that ownership, and taking it anyway is plagiarism with possible legal consequences. Second, the decision skill the exam actually tests: given a reuse scenario, name the legal route that permits it — Creative Commons, open source, or open access — and remember that citation is required no matter which route applies. Third, the wider lens: computing that harms people, sways politics, monitors continuously, or deepens the digital divide raises legal and ethical concerns even when no copyright is involved.

slides.prompt: For a class project, a student remixes three found ingredients: a hit song clipped from a streaming app, a photographer's sunset photo saved from a blog, and a code snippet copied from an online forum.
slides.subprompt: For EACH ingredient: is using it allowed? If not — invent a rule, tag, or deal that WOULD make it allowed. Write your three rulings and your invented fixes.
slides.directions: Write a ruling and a fix for each ingredient. Be ready to defend your inventions.
slides.script: A few minutes, working alone — three borrowed ingredients, three rulings. Most students sense the song is trouble, waver on the photo, and assume the forum code is fair game; the interesting work is the fixes they invent. Typical inventions: 'ask the photographer and get a yes in writing,' 'a tag on the photo that says remixing is welcome,' 'code posted with a note that anyone may use it if they say where it came from.' Those three inventions are, almost word for word, permission, a Creative Commons license, and open source with acknowledgement — students just designed the legal machinery this lesson names. Keep the rulings visible; every section today formalizes one of them.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have 4 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: Your Work Is Property
sections.description: Intellectual property, why easy copying raises ownership concerns, and what plagiarism risks (EK IOC-1.F.1–F.4).

sections.title: Three Legal Routes + One Rule
sections.description: Creative Commons, open source, open access — and why citation is always required (EK IOC-1.F.5–F.7).

sections.title: When Computing Harms
sections.description: Harm to people, political influence, the digital divide, and the CED's three example concerns (EK IOC-1.F.8–F.11).

sections.title: Deep Dive: Decoding a CC Tag
sections.description: The four building blocks of a Creative Commons license and how to read a real tag in the wild.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 5.5 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Today's Guided Notes map: four sections. Three are green and CB-required — ownership and plagiarism, the three legal routes plus the citation rule, then the concerns that reach beyond copyright. The blue deep dive decodes real Creative Commons tags like BY-NC-SA: never tested, immediately useful to anyone who posts or borrows media. Packets stay open all period; a digital copy lives on the Topic 5.5 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com for anyone absent.

slides.label: Your Work Is Property
slides.kicker: Ownership and Plagiarism · Learning Objective IOC-1.F
slides.script: Section one — the property story. Before students can judge anyone else's borrowing, they need the baseline rule: the moment something is created on a computer, it is intellectual property with an owner. Then the tension: digital copies are effortless, which is precisely why ownership, value, and use become concerns. The section ends with the word the Create PT polices — plagiarism.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO IOC-1.F)

terms.definition: Material created on a computer is the intellectual property of the creator or an organization — ownership comes from creating, not from copying (EK IOC-1.F.1).
Using material created by someone else without permission and presenting it as one's own; it may have legal consequences (EK IOC-1.F.4).
A public copyright license enabling free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work — used when the creator wants others to share, use, and build upon it (EK IOC-1.F.5).
Programs that are made freely available and may be redistributed and modified (EK IOC-1.F.5).

slides.script: Four terms carry the whole topic. Intellectual property settles who owns digital material: the creator or an organization, automatically, from the moment of creation. Plagiarism is the violation, and its definition has two required parts students should underline — without permission AND presented as one's own — plus a consequence that reaches past a failing grade into actual law. Creative Commons and open source are the first two legal routes; note that Creative Commons licenses any copyrighted work, while open source is specifically about programs that may be redistributed and modified. Students write every definition in the packet — the standing rule for every vocabulary section.

slides.heading: Ownership in a Copy-Paste World
bullets: Material created on a computer is the intellectual property of the creator or an organization (EK IOC-1.F.1) — your bell-ringer remix is IP too, the moment you make it.
Ease of access and distribution of digitized information raises intellectual property concerns regarding ownership, value, and use (EK IOC-1.F.2) — one click copies what took years to create.
Measures should be taken to safeguard intellectual property (EK IOC-1.F.3): licenses, watermarks, and terms of use all exist to keep control with the creator.
Topic 1.3's rule still applies (LO CRD-2.H): acknowledge any code segment developed by another source, naming the origin or author — the Create PT plagiarism policy enforces exactly this.
slides.script: Four rules build the property story. First, ownership is automatic: everything made on a computer — including student work — is intellectual property from the moment of creation. Second, the digital twist: a song once had to be pressed onto physical discs to be stolen at scale; now distribution is one click, which is why the CED flags ownership, value, and use as concerns. Third, creators respond with safeguards — licenses, watermarks, terms of use. Fourth, connect this to what students already live under: the acknowledgement rule from Topic 1.3 and the Create Performance Task, where an uncredited borrowed code segment can invalidate a score. Same principle, higher stakes.

capture.q: Anything made on a computer belongs, from the instant of creation, to ______.
capture.a: whoever made it — or the organization behind them

capture.q: Digitized information raises ownership, value, and use concerns because it is so ______.
capture.a: cheap to copy and spread at enormous scale

capture.q: Watermarks, licenses, and terms of use are all measures meant to ______.
capture.a: shield a creator's work from being taken over

slides.heading: “Free to View = Free to Use”
slides.misconception: If a song, photo, or article is posted publicly online where anyone can see it for free, then anyone is free to reuse it in their own projects — posting it made it public property.
slides.whyItHappens: The web feels like a commons: no locks, no price tags, right-click and save. Physical intuition says whatever sits out in the open is free for the taking.
slides.correction: Posting transfers nothing: the material remains the creator's intellectual property (EK IOC-1.F.1), and easy access is precisely what raises ownership, value, and use concerns (EK IOC-1.F.2). Reuse needs a legal route — and a citation every time (EK IOC-1.F.6).
slides.example: The bell ringer's sunset photo on a public blog is free to LOOK at. Drop it into a project without permission and present it as your own, and it becomes plagiarism with possible legal consequences (EK IOC-1.F.4).
slides.script: This is the misconception the whole topic exists to kill, and exam distractors are built directly from it. The web's openness is a viewing convenience, not an ownership transfer — a museum with free admission has not given away its paintings. Push students to see that EK IOC-1.F.2 actually predicts this confusion: it is exactly BECAUSE digital access is so easy that ownership, value, and use become live concerns. The repair is a two-part habit: before reusing anything, find the legal route that permits it, then cite the creator regardless. Students write the explanation of why the myth fails in their packets.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Whose Property?
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.F. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Your bell-ringer remix used a song, a photo, and a code snippet. Name the intellectual-property owner of each ingredient — and of the finished remix itself. What does that last answer tell you?

A classmate argues that copying a digital song is harmless since the original owner still has their copy. Using the CED's three concerns — ownership, value, and use — explain what the argument misses.
State the two things that must BOTH be true for a use of someone else's material to count as plagiarism, and name what plagiarism can cost beyond a grade.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.5 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences in the packet. One: each ingredient belongs to its creator — the musician or label, the photographer, the forum programmer — and the remix itself is the student's intellectual property, which usually lands the point that these rules protect students too. Two: the copy-is-harmless argument ignores value — when copies are free and everywhere, the owner's ability to sell or control the work collapses even though they still possess it. Three: without permission AND presented as one's own, and it may carry legal consequences, not just academic ones. A few students will be called on before the next section.

slides.label: Three Legal Routes + One Rule
slides.kicker: Creative Commons, Open Source, Open Access · LO IOC-1.F
slides.script: Section two — the machinery students invented in the bell ringer, now with its real names. Creators who WANT to be copied need a way to say so in advance, and the CED names three: Creative Commons for copyrighted works, open source for programs, open access for research. Then the rule that survives every license: material created by someone else should always be cited. The exam loves swapping these three definitions — this section is where students nail which is which.

slides.heading: Three Legal Routes to Reuse
slides.subheading: Legal ways to use another creator's material (EK IOC-1.F.5) — routes that enabled broad access to digital information (EK IOC-1.F.7).

items.title: Creative Commons
items.summary: A public copyright license enabling free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work — the creator invites sharing, use, and building on it.
items.ekRef: e.g., a CC-tagged photo or song

items.title: Open source
items.summary: Programs made freely available that may be redistributed and modified — the code itself is open to reuse and change.

items.title: Open access
items.summary: Online research output free of any and all restrictions on access, and free of many restrictions on use, such as copyright or license limits.

slides.script: Three cards, three routes, three different targets — and the exam tests students on which is which. Creative Commons is a LICENSE a creator attaches to an otherwise copyrighted work: the photo stays copyrighted, but the tag pre-grants sharing, use, and building upon. Open source is about PROGRAMS: freely available, and — the clause students forget — they may be redistributed and modified, which no ordinary copyright allows. Open access is about RESEARCH output: papers and data readable by anyone, free of access restrictions and many use restrictions. Together, per EK IOC-1.F.7, these three enabled the broad access to digital information students take for granted. Anchor each card to a bell-ringer invention: the photo tag, the forum code note, and — new — the studies their science reports quote.

slides.heading: Permission Is Not Credit
slides.subheading: A license answers 'may I use it?' A citation answers 'who made it?' The exam tests both (EK IOC-1.F.5–F.6).

left.title: Permission — the legal route
left.definition: A license (CC, open source, or open access) grants the RIGHT to use the material
examples: A CC tag on the sunset photo makes the remix legal to build
Open source lets the forum snippet be reused and even modified
No route and no permission — the use itself is the violation

right.title: Credit — the citation
right.definition: Material created by someone other than you should always be cited
examples: Cite even when the license already makes the use legal
For code: an acknowledgement in the documentation, naming origin or author
Skipping credit presents the work as your own — plagiarism's second half

slides.footer: AP TIP: 'It was Creative Commons' answers permission only — a response that stops there and never credits the creator is incomplete.
slides.script: The single most-missed distinction in this topic: a license and a citation answer two different questions. The left column is legality — Creative Commons, open source, and open access each pre-grant the right to use material, so no separate permission is needed. The right column never goes away: per EK IOC-1.F.6, material created by someone other than you should ALWAYS be cited, license or no license. Walk the trap explicitly: a student who uses a CC song and credits nobody has satisfied the left column and failed the right — and remember from plagiarism's definition that presenting work as your own is half of what makes the violation. Legal route plus credit, every time.

capture.q: A Creative Commons or open-source license settles only the question of ______.
capture.a: whether you are allowed to reuse the work at all

capture.q: Even when a license makes the use fully legal, you must still ______.
capture.a: name the original creator every single time

capture.q: Together, the three routes of EK IOC-1.F.5 have enabled ______.
capture.a: wide public reach for the world's digital content

slides.heading: Route or Violation? Five Cases
slides.context: For each use, name the legal route (Creative Commons, open source, open access) or the violation — then what fixes it (EK IOC-1.F.4–F.6).
rows: Quoting long passages of a paywalled novel on a fan site — no permission, no credit
Get the author's permission AND cite the source
Building an app on a program licensed for free redistribution and modification
Already legal — cite the project and its authors (EK IOC-1.F.6)
Using a photographer's CC-tagged image in a school slideshow
Already legal — credit the photographer as the tag asks
Quoting a restriction-free online research study in a science report, with citation
Already legal — the report cites the researchers
Pasting a classmate's Create PT code into your own submission as yours
Acknowledge the origin in the documentation — or write your own
slides.script: Five cases, one two-step test each: is there a route, and is there credit? Case one has neither — paywalled text, no permission, no credit — plagiarism, and the fix needs both halves. Case two is the open-source giveaway: 'free redistribution and modification' is the definition, and the remaining duty is citation. Case three is Creative Commons doing its job — the tag pre-granted use, credit completes it. Case four is open access: a restriction-free study, quoted WITH citation, fully clean. Case five is the one that decides scores in May: borrowed Create PT code presented as one's own is plagiarism even between friends, and the fix is the Topic 1.3 acknowledgement — origin and author, in the documentation. Students complete the blanked cells before answers are revealed.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Run the Routes
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.F. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: Return to your three bell-ringer rulings. For each ingredient, name the real legal route (or needed permission) that matches the fix you invented — and state the one duty no route removes.
A friend says: 'The library I used is open source, so I don't have to mention it anywhere in my project.' Which half of the friend's claim is right, which half is wrong, and per which rules?
In one sentence each, distinguish open source from open access — name what KIND of material each route covers and what it lets you do.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.5 page.
slides.script: Six minutes, full sentences. One: the song needs the owner's permission or a CC license, the photo matches Creative Commons, the forum snippet matches open source — and no route removes the duty to cite. Two: the friend is right that open source grants legal use and modification, wrong that it erases credit — EK IOC-1.F.5 gives the permission, EK IOC-1.F.6 requires the citation anyway. Three: open source covers programs and permits redistribution and modification; open access covers research output and removes access restrictions plus many use restrictions. Listen for students who can now argue in route names instead of vibes — that is the skill the quiz rewards.

slides.label: When Computing Harms
slides.kicker: Harm, Politics, and the Digital Divide · LO IOC-1.F
slides.script: Section three widens the lens past copyright. LO IOC-1.F says the USE of computing raises legal and ethical concerns — and property is only the first kind. Computing aimed at people can harm them; computing woven into social and political life inherits those stakes; and unequal access — the digital divide from Topic 5.2 — is itself an ethical concern. The CED closes with three named examples students should be able to recognize on sight.

slides.heading: Legal and Ethical, Beyond Copyright
bullets: As with any technology or medium, using computing to harm individuals or groups of people raises legal and ethical concerns (EK IOC-1.F.8).
Computing can play a role in social and political issues, which in turn often raises legal and ethical concerns (EK IOC-1.F.9).
The digital divide raises ethical concerns around computing (EK IOC-1.F.10) — Topic 5.2's access gap is not just unequal, it is a question of right and wrong.
The CED's example concerns (EK IOC-1.F.11): software for digital media downloads and streaming, algorithms that include bias, and devices that continuously monitor and analyze activity.
slides.script: Four statements finish the learning objective. First, the general principle: computing is a technology like any other — aimed at people, it can harm them, and harm brings both law and ethics into play. Second, computing now runs elections, protests, and public debate, so it inherits the stakes of social and political life. Third, the digital divide reappears — students met it in Topic 5.2 as a description of unequal access; here the CED classifies that gap as an ethical concern. Fourth, the three examples worth recognizing on the exam: streaming and download software colliding with media ownership, biased algorithms from Topic 5.3 deciding who gets loans or interviews, and always-on devices quietly logging daily life. Every one of them is a familiar product, which is the point.

capture.q: Turning computing against individuals or groups raises questions of ______.
capture.a: law along with right and wrong, together

capture.q: The digital divide belongs in this topic because unequal access is ______.
capture.a: a fairness problem, not merely a tech gap

capture.q: A smart speaker makes the CED's example list because it ______.
capture.a: constantly gathers and studies what people do

slides.heading: Deep Dive: Decoding a CC Tag
slides.subheading: The CED defines Creative Commons but never asks you to read a real tag — decoding one is beyond the exam and worth two minutes.

left.title: The four building blocks
left.definition: Every CC tag combines four switches the creator sets
examples: BY — credit the creator (part of every CC license)
ND — no derivatives: share it unchanged, no remixes
SA — share-alike: remixes must carry this same license

right.title: Reading one in the wild
right.definition: CC BY-NC-SA: credit me, no selling, share remixes alike
examples: A BY-ND song could NOT be legally remixed — even with credit
BY alone is the most open: remix, even sell, just credit
The bell-ringer remix needs tags whose switches allow editing

slides.footer: Deep dive only — the exam names Creative Commons as a legal route; it never asks you to parse BY/NC/ND/SA switches.
slides.script: A deep dive for the students who post and borrow media every day — this is beyond what the exam requires. Creative Commons is not one license but a menu built from four switches: BY requires credit and appears in every license; NC forbids commercial use; ND forbids derivatives; SA forces remixes to inherit the same license. Read one aloud: BY-NC-SA means credit the creator, no selling, and any remix ships under identical terms. The punchline for the bell ringer: not every CC tag would save the remix project — an ND tag forbids the very editing a remix is. Fast finishers can decide which single tag they would put on their own portfolio work, and why.

capture.q: The one switch found in every Creative Commons license is ______.
capture.a: BY, which demands the maker be acknowledged

capture.q: An ND-tagged song cannot legally be remixed, since ND forbids ______.
capture.a: publishing altered versions of the work

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Concerns on Sight
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.F. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: A hiring app quietly ranks applicants using an algorithm trained mostly on past employees. Name which EK IOC-1.F.11 example this matches and state one legal and one ethical concern it raises.
A city moves a public benefit to online-only applications. Using the CED's language, explain which concern this raises and for whom — even though no one's property was touched.
Streaming and download software appears on the CED's concern list. Connect it to Section 1: which intellectual-property concerns does effortless streaming and downloading create?
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.5 page.
slides.script: Five minutes, full sentences. One: the hiring app is the biased-algorithm example — legally it risks discrimination claims; ethically it can silently repeat past unfairness at scale. Two: online-only benefits raise the digital divide as an ethical concern — residents without devices or connectivity lose access to something they are entitled to. Three: streaming and download software is EK IOC-1.F.2 in action — when distribution is effortless, ownership, value, and use of the media all come under pressure, which is why that software heads the CED's example list. Answers are reviewed as a class before the traps slide.

slides.heading: Common AP Traps
slides.subheading: Three ways Topic 5.5 loses points on the exam — one minute now, real points in May.

strategies.name: Free to view ≠ free to use
strategies.text: Public posting is not permission — the work stays the creator's intellectual property, and reuse needs a legal route. 'Online means public domain' is a distractor.

strategies.name: Three routes, not one
strategies.text: Creative Commons licenses a copyrighted WORK; open source frees PROGRAMS for redistribution and modification; open access frees RESEARCH output. Items swap the definitions.

strategies.name: Legal use still needs credit
strategies.text: A license answers permission, never credit: material created by someone else should ALWAYS be cited. 'It was CC, so no citation needed' is a trap answer, not a rule.

slides.script: One minute, three traps. Trap one: the free-to-view reflex — distractors dress it up as 'publicly available,' 'posted openly,' or 'no watermark,' and none of those grant reuse rights. Trap two: definition swapping — the exam's favorite move is describing open source and labeling it open access, or vice versa; students should match the route to its target: works, programs, research. Trap three: the license-erases-credit myth — EK IOC-1.F.6 has no exceptions, so any option claiming a license removes the need to cite is wrong. Students write each trap in their own words in the packet.

slides.heading: Legal and Ethical Concerns, in One Slide
points: Material created on a computer is intellectual property — and easy digital access raises ownership, value, and use concerns worth safeguarding against (EK IOC-1.F.1–F.3).
Plagiarism = someone else's material, without permission, presented as your own — with possible legal consequences (EK IOC-1.F.4).
Three legal routes to reuse: Creative Commons (licensed works), open source (modifiable programs), open access (unrestricted research) — together enabling broad access (EK IOC-1.F.5, F.7).
Material created by someone other than you should ALWAYS be cited — no license removes that duty (EK IOC-1.F.6).
Computing that harms people, shapes politics, monitors continuously, embeds bias, or deepens the digital divide raises legal and ethical concerns (EK IOC-1.F.8–F.11).
slides.next: Topic 5.6, Safe Computing: from other people's rights to your own — PII, phishing, encryption, and how your personal data is collected and protected.
slides.script: The whole topic in one slide. Creation makes property; effortless copying is why ownership, value, and use are live concerns; plagiarism is the two-part violation with legal teeth. Creators who want sharing use one of three routes — Creative Commons for works, open source for programs, open access for research — and citation survives every one of them. Past copyright, computing that harms, politicks, monitors, discriminates, or excludes raises the same two flavors of concern: legal and ethical. Next class opens with the Topic 5.5 quiz, then Topic 5.6 turns the lens around: protecting your own data. Before packing up, students run the exit check below — any unchecked box is tonight's review target on the Topic 5.5 page. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.
ican: explain how easy digital copying raises ownership, value, and use concerns (LO IOC-1.F)
define plagiarism by its two parts and name its possible consequences (LO IOC-1.F)
match a reuse case to Creative Commons, open source, or open access (LO IOC-1.F)
explain why citing is required even when a use is legal (LO IOC-1.F)

quiz.title: Topic 5.5 — Legal and Ethical Concerns — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 7 questions, 12 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A student finds a photographer's sunset photo on a free-to-view public blog, saves it, and uses it as her class-project cover without permission or credit. This use is best described as:
options: A legal use, since posting an image on a publicly viewable blog releases it from ownership
Plagiarism — the image remains the photographer's intellectual property, and it was used without permission and presented as the student's own
A legal use under open access, since the blog placed no restrictions on viewing the image
Plagiarism, but with no possible legal consequences as long as the project is for a class
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.F.1 makes the image the photographer's intellectual property regardless of where it is posted, and EK IOC-1.F.4 defines this exact combination — someone else's material, no permission, presented as one's own — as plagiarism that may have legal consequences. A is the free-to-view misconception: easy access raises IP concerns (EK IOC-1.F.2); it never transfers ownership. C misapplies open access, which covers online research output free of restrictions — not a photographer's blog image. D names the violation but invents a classroom exemption; EK IOC-1.F.4 says plagiarism MAY have legal consequences, with no school carve-out.

questions.stem: Which statements about intellectual property are true? I. Material created on a computer belongs to its creator or organization. II. Using another's material without permission, as your own, can have legal consequences. III. Posting material online ends its ownership.
options: I only
II and III only
I, II, and III
I and II only
questions.rationale: Statement I is EK IOC-1.F.1 nearly verbatim, and Statement II is EK IOC-1.F.4's definition of plagiarism plus its consequence. Statement III is false — no EK strips ownership at the moment of posting; EK IOC-1.F.2 says ease of access raises CONCERNS about ownership, value, and use precisely because ownership persists. That falsehood eliminates B and C. A undersells the topic by rejecting II, whose two-part plagiarism definition and legal consequence come straight from the CED.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes open access?
options: Online research output free of any and all restrictions on access and free of many restrictions on use, such as copyright or license restrictions
Programs that are made freely available and may be redistributed and modified
A public copyright license that enables the free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work
A rule stating that material created by someone other than you should always be cited
questions.rationale: A is the CED's open-access definition: online RESEARCH output, free of all access restrictions and many use restrictions. The distractors are the classic definition swap the exam runs on this EK: B is open source (programs, redistributable and modifiable), C is Creative Commons (a public copyright license for an otherwise copyrighted work), and D is the citation rule from EK IOC-1.F.6 — a duty that applies across all three routes, not a route itself. Students must attach each definition to its target: works, programs, research.

questions.stem: A programmer wants anyone to download her graphing library free, redistribute it, and modify its code for their own projects. Which legal route matches this goal?
options: Open access, since the library will be available online without restrictions
Standard copyright with no license, which automatically permits modification once code is published
Open source, which makes programs freely available and allows them to be redistributed and modified
Creative Commons, the only route under which a program may ever be legally changed
questions.rationale: The programmer's three goals — freely available, redistributable, modifiable — restate the CED's open-source definition word for word, and the material is a PROGRAM, open source's specific target. A confuses the routes: open access covers online research output, not software libraries. B inverts copyright law — publication grants no modification rights; that is exactly why explicit licenses exist. D is false on two counts: Creative Commons is the CED's route for otherwise copyrighted works generally, and 'only route' contradicts open source's explicit permission to modify programs.

questions.stem: A student legally uses a Creative Commons–licensed song in a class documentary, and the license permits the reuse. Which statement best describes the remaining obligation?
options: None — a valid license removes any need to mention the songwriter
The student should still cite the songwriter, as material created by someone else should always be cited
The student must pay the songwriter a royalty before the documentary is shown
The student must re-license the entire documentary under the same Creative Commons terms
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.F.6 is unconditional: the use of material created by someone other than you should always be cited — a license settles permission, never credit. A is the license-erases-credit myth, the exact trap this EK exists to close. C contradicts EK IOC-1.F.5: Creative Commons enables the FREE distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work, so no royalty is owed for a permitted use. D smuggles in share-alike, a feature of only some CC licenses — the stem says the license permits the reuse, and no EK imposes automatic re-licensing.

questions.stem: A family installs a smart speaker that continuously listens to and analyzes household conversations to improve its recommendations. Which statement best describes why such devices raise concern?
options: Computing devices that collect and analyze data by continuously monitoring activities can raise legal and ethical concerns
Any device that records audio in a home automatically violates intellectual property law
Smart speakers may not legally be sold unless their software is released as open source
The concern is purely technical — constant listening wastes bandwidth rather than raising ethical questions
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.F.11 names three example concerns, and the third is this device exactly: computing devices that collect and analyze data by continuously monitoring activities. A restates it. B misfires on category — recording household audio implicates privacy and monitoring, not intellectual property, which covers created material. C invents a law: open source is a voluntary legal route (EK IOC-1.F.5), never a sales requirement. D shrinks an ethics question to a bandwidth question; the CED's concern is the continuous collection and analysis of people's activity, a legal and ethical matter.

questions.stem: A city moves all housing-assistance applications to a smartphone-only app. Residents without smartphones or reliable Internet — often the lowest-income households — can no longer apply easily. Which concern does this raise?
options: An intellectual property concern, since the city now owns each resident's application data
No concern — moving a public service online improves access equally for every group
A plagiarism concern, since the app was likely built from uncited open-source code
An ethical concern raised by the digital divide, which the smartphone-only rule deepens
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.F.10 states that the digital divide raises ethical concerns around computing. A smartphone-only public service excludes exactly the residents on the wrong side of that divide, turning unequal access into unequal rights — the ethical concern the EK names. A misuses intellectual property, which is about created material's ownership, not application records. B assumes online delivery benefits everyone equally — the stem's excluded households refute it. C invents facts not in the scenario and misstates the rule: open-source reuse is legal (EK IOC-1.F.5), though it should be cited (EK IOC-1.F.6).

guide.title: Topic 5.5 — Legal and Ethical Concerns — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing · Topic 5.5 · 1 class period + assessment block
overview: Topic 5.5 turns Big Idea 5's lens onto ownership and responsibility. Students learn that anything created on a computer is intellectual property, that effortless digital copying is exactly why ownership, value, and use become concerns, and that plagiarism — someone else's material, without permission, presented as one's own — can carry legal consequences. The heart of the topic is the CED's three legal routes to reuse (Creative Commons, open source, open access), the rule that survives all of them (always cite), and the wider concerns computing raises beyond copyright: harm to people, political influence, biased algorithms, always-on monitoring, and the digital divide.

The through-line is the bell ringer: students remix a song, a photo, and a code snippet, rule on each ingredient, and invent the fix that would make it allowed — thereby designing permission, Creative Commons, and open source before hearing the names. The topic also closes a loop begun in Topic 1.3: the acknowledgement rule for borrowed code (LO CRD-2.H) is the same principle the Create Performance Task's plagiarism policy enforces, now generalized to every kind of digital material.
los: LO IOC-1.F — Explain how the use of computing can raise legal and ethical concerns.
eks: EK IOC-1.F.1 — Material created on a computer is the intellectual property of the creator or an organization.
EK IOC-1.F.2 — Ease of access and distribution of digitized information raises intellectual property concerns regarding ownership, value, and use.
EK IOC-1.F.3 — Measures should be taken to safeguard intellectual property.
EK IOC-1.F.4 — The use of material created by someone else without permission and presented as one's own is plagiarism and may have legal consequences.

EK IOC-1.F.5 — Some examples of legal ways to use materials created by someone else include: • Creative Commons — a public copyright license that enables the free distribution of an otherwise copyrighted work. This is used when the content creator wants to give others the right to share, use, and build upon the work they have created. • open source — programs that are made freely available and may be redistributed and modified • open access — online research output free of any and all restrictions on access and free of many restrictions on use, such as copyright or license restrictions
EK IOC-1.F.6 — The use of material created by someone other than you should always be cited.
EK IOC-1.F.7 — Creative Commons, open source, and open access have enabled broad access to digital information.
EK IOC-1.F.8 — As with any technology or medium, using computing to harm individuals or groups of people raises legal and ethical concerns.
EK IOC-1.F.9 — Computing can play a role in social and political issues, which in turn often raises legal and ethical concerns.
EK IOC-1.F.10 — The digital divide raises ethical concerns around computing.

EK IOC-1.F.11 — Computing innovations can raise legal and ethical concerns. Some examples of these include: the development of software that allows access to digital media downloads and streaming; the development of algorithms that include bias; the existence of computing devices that collect and analyze data by continuously monitoring activities.
materials: Printed Topic 5.5 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Optional: 3-4 real Creative Commons license tags (BY, BY-ND, BY-NC-SA) printed or on screen for the deep dive.
prereqs: Topic 1.3 (Program Design and Development): students should already know the acknowledgement rule for borrowed code — any segment developed by another source is acknowledged in the program documentation with its origin or author (LO CRD-2.H). Topic 5.5 generalizes that rule to every kind of digital material and names the stakes. Topics 5.2 (Digital Divide) and 5.3 (Computing Bias) supply the access gap and biased-algorithm ideas that EK IOC-1.F.10 and F.11 reframe as ethical concerns.

pacing.focus: Intellectual property and plagiarism, the three legal routes plus the citation rule, and computing's wider legal-ethical concerns (LO IOC-1.F)

segments.segment: QOTD (BI5/T5.5/Q1) + bell ringer (the three-ingredient remix project)
Your work is property: vocab, ownership in a copy-paste world, free-to-view misconception (EK IOC-1.F.1–F.4)
Three legal routes + the citation rule: routes grid, permission vs. credit, route-or-violation table (EK IOC-1.F.5–F.7)
Stop and Think: run the routes
When computing harms: harm, politics, digital divide, the CED's example concerns (EK IOC-1.F.8–F.11)
Deep dive: decoding a CC tag (optional enrichment)
Stop and Think: concerns on sight + Common AP Traps + exit check

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 5.5 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “Free to view = free to use”
misconceptions.myth: If material is posted publicly online where anyone can see it for free, anyone may reuse it — posting made it public property.
misconceptions.reality: Posting transfers nothing. The material remains the creator's intellectual property (EK IOC-1.F.1), and easy access is exactly what raises ownership, value, and use concerns (EK IOC-1.F.2). Reuse requires a legal route — permission, Creative Commons, open source, or open access — plus a citation every time (EK IOC-1.F.6).

misconceptions.heading: “Creative Commons, open source, and open access are the same thing”
misconceptions.myth: The three routes are interchangeable labels for 'free stuff on the Internet,' so naming any one of them earns the point.
misconceptions.reality: Each route has a distinct target and grant (EK IOC-1.F.5): Creative Commons is a public copyright license on an otherwise copyrighted WORK; open source is PROGRAMS that are freely available and may be redistributed and modified; open access is online RESEARCH output free of access restrictions. Exam items swap these definitions deliberately — students must match route to target.

misconceptions.heading: “A license means no citation is needed”
misconceptions.myth: Once a Creative Commons or open-source license makes a use legal, crediting the creator is optional courtesy.
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.F.6 is unconditional: material created by someone other than you should ALWAYS be cited. A license answers permission; citation answers credit. Skipping credit re-creates half of plagiarism's definition — presenting the work as one's own (EK IOC-1.F.4) — and on the Create PT it can invalidate a score.

support: Give the two-question test as a sentence frame for every scenario: 'The route that permits this use is ______, and the credit still owed is ______.' Students fill it before writing free-form answers.

Anchor every case back to the three bell-ringer ingredients: 'Is this a song problem (needs permission or a license), a photo problem (look for the CC tag), or a code problem (open source + acknowledgement)?'
extension: Have deep-dive students license their own portfolio piece: choose a CC tag (BY, BY-ND, BY-NC-SA, or BY only), defend the switch choices in two sentences, and predict what a stranger could legally do with the work.

Challenge students to find the tension inside EK IOC-1.F.11: streaming software raises IP concerns, yet Creative Commons, open source, and open access (EK IOC-1.F.7) exist to ENABLE broad access. Have them articulate the difference between access a creator granted and access nobody granted.

discussion: Memes are remixes of images their makers never licensed. Is meme culture mass plagiarism, a new kind of Creative Commons that nobody wrote down, or something else? Where should the law draw the line?

Your school could adopt monitoring software that watches every student screen to keep classes on task. Which of the CED's concerns does that raise, and what rule would make the trade acceptable — if any?

Open access says research should be free to everyone; journals reply that reviewing and publishing costs money someone must pay. Who should pay for knowledge — readers, universities, governments — and what happens to the digital divide under each answer?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
